title: Isaiah 35
subtitle: The Highway of Holiness: The Desert Will Rejoice

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “The Blind Will See, the Deaf Will Hear, the Lame Will Leap”

heb.gloss: to blossom / to burst into bloom
heb.paragraph: "The desert and the parched land will be glad; the wilderness will rejoice and blossom (pāraḥ). Like the crocus, it will burst into bloom" (v.1). THE mirror-chapter to ch.34: devastation answered by restoration. "They will see the glory of the LORD, the splendour of our God" (v.2). "Strengthen the feeble hands, steady the knees that give way" (v.3) — Hebrews 12:12 quotes this. "Then will the eyes of the blind be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped. Then will the lame leap like a deer, and the mute tongue shout for joy" (vv.5–6). Jesus quotes this list to John the Baptist’s disciples as proof of his messiahship: "The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the deaf hear" (Matt 11:5; Luke 7:22).

notes.note: "The desert will rejoice and blossom. They will see the glory of the LORD." After ch.34’s devastation: blooming desert. The same land cursed in ch.34 is redeemed in ch.35.

"Strengthen the feeble hands, steady the knees that give way. Say to those with fearful hearts: Be strong, do not fear; your God will come." Hebrews 12:12 quotes vv.3–4. The pastoral instruction: encourage the weak before the miracle arrives.

"Then the blind will see, the deaf will hear, the lame will leap, the mute will shout." The four reversals. Jesus points to these as credentials (Matt 11:5). His miracles are not random; they fulfil Isaiah 35.

refs.note: The highway in the wilderness — chapter 35 anticipates chapter 40's 'comfort' theme.
Jesus cites Isaiah 35 signs: blind see, lame walk — messianic authentication.
The same citation in Luke: John's disciples told to report what they see.
No more death or mourning — the new creation fulfills Isaiah's vision of sorrow fled away.

hist.context: Chapter 35 is the glorious counterpart to chapter 34: where Edom becomes wilderness, the wilderness becomes garden. The 'glory of Lebanon' and 'splendor of Carmel' (v.2) — the most beautiful landscapes — will adorn the desert. This is new-creation language: the curse reversed, Eden restored. The healing catalogue (vv.5-6) — blind seeing, deaf hearing, lame leaping, mute singing — became definitive of messianic expectation. Jesus' miracles intentionally fulfilled this list.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the dramatic contrast with chapter 34: Edom becomes desert; the desert becomes Lebanon. Creation itself participates in redemption — the wilderness 'rejoices' at Yahweh's coming.

The healing list specifies what 'salvation' means: physical wholeness, sensory restoration, full participation in worship ('the mute tongue shouts for joy'). Oswalt sees this as the template for Jesus' ministry.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads chapter 35 as the theological climax of First Isaiah (1-39) before the historical appendix (36-39). The judgment section ends not with Edom's desolation but with Zion's restoration. Hope has the last word.

Childs notes these verses became the standard 'messianic profile' in Second Temple Judaism. When Jesus cited them, he was claiming to fulfill specifically Isaianic expectations. The text shaped messianic hope.

sections.header: Verses 7–10 — “A Highway Will Be There, Called the Way of Holiness”

heb.paragraph: "A highway (məsillâ) will be there; it will be called the Way of Holiness. It will be for those who walk on that Way. The unclean will not journey on it; wicked fools will not go about on it" (v.8). The burning sand becomes a pool; the thirsty ground becomes springs (v.7). No lion, no ravenous beast (v.9). "And those the LORD has rescued will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee away" (v.10). The last verse of ch.35 is the first taste of what ch.40 will elaborate. Sorrow flees; joy overtakes. The highway leads home.

notes.note: "A highway called the Way of Holiness." Not just a road but a holy road. Access is moral, not geographical. The highway theology: 11:16 (second exodus), 19:23 (Egypt-Assyria), 35:8 (holiness), 40:3 (voice in the wilderness).

"Those the LORD has rescued will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Sorrow and sighing will flee away." Isaiah 51:11 repeats this verse nearly verbatim. The refrain of the redeemed. Joy pursues them; sorrow retreats.

refs.note: A highway for the remnant from Assyria — the earlier highway promise.
'Prepare the way for the LORD' — the highway through the wilderness.
'Build up the highway; remove the stones' — the same return-road imagery.
'Nothing impure will ever enter' — the holy way's exclusion of uncleanness.

hist.context: The 'Way of Holiness' (v.8) is the highway home for the exiles — safe, clean, protected. 'Fools' (v.8) cannot wander onto it; lions cannot threaten it. The return is joyful procession with singing (v.10), not fearful flight. 'Everlasting joy' crowns the returnees; 'sorrow and sighing' flee. This becomes the paradigm for all future salvation: exodus, return, and final redemption all follow the same pattern — God making a way through the wilderness.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt sees the 'Way of Holiness' as the theological counterpart to the political highways Judah's leaders tried to build to Egypt. God's highway leads home; human highways lead to defeat.

The final verse ('everlasting joy...sorrow and sighing will flee') is quoted nearly verbatim in 51:11. Oswalt notes this creates literary frame for Second Isaiah — the vision that opens chapter 35 closes chapter 51.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs emphasizes the canonical function: chapter 35 prepares for chapters 40-66. The themes announced here (highway, return, joy, salvation) are developed at length in Second Isaiah. The book has unity despite its parts.

The 'ransomed of the LORD' language anticipates chapter 43's ransom theology. Childs sees 35 as transitional: concluding First Isaiah while opening Second Isaiah's themes. It's the hinge of the whole book.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "Those the LORD has rescued will return…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAnd a highway will be there; it will be called the Way of Holiness; it will be for those who walk on that Way. The unclean will not journey on it; wicked fools will not go about on it.ESVAnd a highway shall be there, and it shall be called the Way of Holiness; the unclean shall not pass over it. It shall belong to those who walk on the way; even if they are fools, they shall not go astray.NIVNo lion will be there, nor any ravenous beast; they will not be found there. But only the redeemed will walk there,ESVNo lion shall be there, nor shall any ravenous beast come up on it; they shall not be found there, but the redeemed shall walk there.

rec.text: ""The desert will rejoice and blossom. They will see the glory of the LORD." After ch.34’s devastation: blooming desert. The same land cursed in ch.34 is redeemed in ch.35."

rows.label: The Highway Home — 35:7–10
rows.text: "The WAY OF HOLINESS." No lion, no beast. "Sorrow and sighing will FLEE AWAY." Joy overtakes.

hebtext.gloss: to blossom / to burst into bloom
hebtext.note: "The desert and the parched land will be glad; the wilderness will rejoice and blossom (pāraḥ). Like the crocus, it will burst into bloom" (v.1). THE mirror-chapter to ch.34: devastation answered by restoration. "They will see the glory of the LORD, the splendour of our God" (v.2). "Strengthen the feeble hands, steady the knees that give way" (v.3) — Hebrews 12:12 quotes this. "Then will the eyes of the blind be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped. Then will the lame leap like a deer, and the mute tongue shout for joy" (vv.5–6). Jesus quotes this list to John the Baptist’s disciples as proof of his messiahship: "The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the deaf hear" (Matt 11:5; Luke 7:22).

"A highway (məsillâ) will be there; it will be called the Way of Holiness. It will be for those who walk on that Way. The unclean will not journey on it; wicked fools will not go about on it" (v.8). The burning sand becomes a pool; the thirsty ground becomes springs (v.7). No lion, no ravenous beast (v.9). "And those the LORD has rescued will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee away" (v.10). The last verse of ch.35 is the first taste of what ch.40 will elaborate. Sorrow flees; joy overtakes. The highway leads home.

themes.note: Mercy AND Prophecy peak at 10: the four reversals (Matt 11:5), the highway of holiness, sorrow flees. Holiness: the Way itself is holy. Ch.35 is the positive mirror of ch.34.

coaching.tip: Chapter 35 is the deliberate inverse of chapter 34. Desolation becomes flowering; judgment becomes healing. The blind see, the deaf hear, the lame leap (vv. 5–6). Isaiah pairs these chapters to make a theological point: God's judgment and God's salvation are not competing impulses but complementary acts. You cannot read one without the other. Destruction clears ground for restoration.

'A highway will be there; it will be called the Way of Holiness' (v. 8). Isaiah returns to the road metaphor — the same highway prepared in 40:3 ('make straight in the desert a highway for our God'). The desert road becomes Isaiah's signature image for God's redemptive action: clearing obstacles, leveling terrain, making a path home. John the Baptist will stand on this road.
